title: Small Modular Reactors vs Large Nuclear: The Economics of Scale | by Shb | Medium
text:
Small Modular Reactors vs Large Nuclear: The Economics of Scale
Small modular reactors (SMRs) occupy a curious position in contemporary energy policy because they are rarely discussed in proportion to the problem they are meant to address. They are alternately promoted as a panacea for decarbonisation or dismissed as an economic cul-de-sac. Both views obscure a simpler reality.
SMRs sit at the intersection of physical law, system economics and institutional constraint. Their merits and limits become visible only when they are placed alongside the other technologies competing to displace fossil fuels – and when the mathematics governing each role is kept firmly in view.
The starting point remains scale. Heavy industrial systems reward size. This is not a nuclear peculiarity, but a regularity long observed across refineries, chemical plants and power stations. Engineers formalised it decades ago in what became known as the “six-tenths rule”: capital cost tends to rise with capacity raised to a power of about 0.6. The relationship was codified in mid-20th-century chemical-engineering practice and remains a standard tool in preliminary cost estimation, most famously set out in texts such as Plant Design and Economics for Chemical Engineers. It reflects geometry rather than optimism. Volume grows faster than surface area; many systems – control rooms, licensing effort, site works, supervision – are lumpy rather than divisible. Doubling output rarely requires doubling everything else.
Nuclear power stations, for all their regulatory singularity, remain assemblies of pressure vessels, turbines, heat exchangers and concrete. Their costs follow the same sub-linear scaling observed across heavy industry: capital cost rises roughly with capacity raised to the power of 0.6. Shrinking a reactor therefore reduces cost, but by much less than proportionally. A reactor one-twelfth the size of a gigawatt-scale unit costs not one-twelfth as much, but about 12^{0.6}, or roughly one-quarter.
To recover the lost output, however, twelve such units must be built. Even if the six-tenths rule applied perfectly, this would imply a total cost around 2.5 to 3 times that of the single large reactor. In practice, the penalty is larger. When multiple small reactors are deployed on separate sites, many costs – containment structures, safety systems, staffing, licensing and site works – are duplicated rather than shared. These elements scale closer to unit count than to capacity, pushing the effective scaling exponent toward 0.9 (consequence of duplicated fixed costs) . Under that assumption, twelve small reactors delivering the same power as one large unit cost roughly twice as much overall.
Manufacturing learning vs scale: step-by-step check (NuScale example, 12-unit case)
	1.	Capital cost in heavy industry scales sub-linearly with capacity:
Cost ∝ Capacity^α, where α ≈ 0.6 for a single integrated plant.
	2.	Electrical capacity of a standard large reactor:
C_large_capacity = 924 MW.
	3.	Electrical capacity of one NuScale SMR module:
C_SMR_capacity = 77 MW.
	4.	Number of NuScale modules required to match large reactor output:
N = 924 / 77 = 12 modules.
	5.	Capital cost of one large reactor (normalised reference):
C_large = (924)^0.6.
	6.	Capital cost of one NuScale module implied by scaling:
C_SMR = (77)^0.6.
	7.	Relative cost of one NuScale module compared with large reactor:
C_SMR / C_large = (77 / 924)^0.6 = (1 / 12)^0.6 ≈ 0.28.
	8.	Total capital cost of 12 NuScale modules under optimistic single-plant scaling:
C_fleet = 12 × 0.28 ≈ 3.4 × C_large.
	9.	In practice, twelve reactors duplicate containment structures, safety systems, control rooms, operating crews, security arrangements, licensing effort and site preparation. These costs are triggered by the presence of a reactor rather than its electrical output and therefore decline far more slowly than capacity as reactors get smaller.
	10.	Effective scaling for a multi-reactor fleet therefore shifts from capacity-based scaling to unit-count scaling:
Cost ∝ N^α_eff, with α_eff ≈ 0.8–0.9.
	11.	Total NuScale fleet cost under duplication-dominated scaling:
12^0.8 ≈ 7.0
12^0.9 ≈ 9.0.
	12.	Cost of one large reactor expressed on the same output basis using capacity scaling:
12^0.6 ≈ 4.4.
	13.	Resulting NuScale capital-cost penalty at equal output:
7.0 / 4.4 ≈ 1.6×
9.0 / 4.4 ≈ 2.0×.
	14.	Required cost reduction from learning to achieve parity with the large reactor:
≈ 38–50%, derived directly from the inverse of the cost penalty above
(1 − 1/1.6 ≈ 38%, 1 − 1/2.0 = 50%).
	15.	Learning-by-doing represented by Wright’s law:
Cost ∝ (Cumulative production)^(-β).
	16.	Cost reduction after n doublings with learning rate LR:
(1 − LR)^n.
	17.	With LR = 20% per doubling (extremely optimistic for nuclear):
0.8^n ≈ 0.6 → n ≈ 2.5 → 6–8 identical NuScale modules required.
	18.	With LR = 10% per doubling (upper bound for heavy industry):
0.9^n ≈ 0.6 → n ≈ 5 → 32 identical modules required.
	19.	With LR = 5% per doubling (closer to nuclear experience):
0.95^n ≈ 0.6 → n ≈ 10 → >1,000 modules required.
	20.	Approximate share of NuScale capital cost subject to factory learning:
≈ 30% (modules, controls, factory QA).
	21.	Maximum theoretical cost reduction if that share fell to zero:
0.3 × 100% = 30%.
	22.	Required reduction (38–50%) exceeds achievable reduction from learning (≤30%).
	23.	Learning reduces cost levels; scale determines cost slope.
	24.	Large reactors also benefit from learning, from a lower starting cost base.
	25.	Conclusion: for a 12-module NuScale deployment, learning narrows but does not eliminate the scale penalty.This result does not rest on pessimism. It follows from abandoning vertical scale. Nor is it overturned by repetition. Learning effects are real, but they operate differently. The idea that costs fall as cumulative production rises – often called a learning curve or experience curve – originated not in energy policy but in aircraft manufacturing. In 1936, Theodore Paul Wright observed that each doubling of aircraft production reduced labour hours by a predictable percentage, a relationship later generalised into what economists call Wright’s law. Learning curves have since been documented across shipbuilding, photovoltaics, batteries and semiconductors.
Their strength, however, depends on what is being learned. Learning is most powerful where production is repetitive, automation is feasible, and redesign is unconstrained. It is weaker where work is site-specific, inspection dominates cost, and regulation freezes designs. Nuclear construction sits closer to shipbuilding than to electronics. Repetition can lower costs and reduce risk, but it tends to shift the cost curve downward rather than flatten it. Scale still determines the slope. Large reactors learn as well; they simply start from a lower cost per kilowatt. The coexistence of these two effects – sub-linear scaling and learning-by-doing – is not a contradiction. One governs how cost changes with size; the other governs where the curve sits.
If this were the entire story, SMRs would indeed look like an inferior substitute for conventional nuclear power. But that conclusion rests on an implicit assumption: that SMRs are competing with large reactors to provide cheap baseload electricity. In practice, that is not the contest that determines their appeal. The alternatives they are most often weighed against are assets whose economics are shaped not by capital efficiency, but by fuel cost, volatility and system constraint.
Much of the remaining fossil generation in advanced power systems sits at the margins rather than the centre. Gas peaker plants operate for limited hours, often concentrated in the early evening – roughly between 4 pm and 9 pm – when demand rises, solar output collapses and wind cannot be relied upon. Diesel generators supply remote communities and isolated industrial sites where grid reinforcement is costly or impractical. Fossil-fired boilers provide high-temperature industrial heat where electrification is difficult. These assets exist not because they are efficient, but because they are dependable.
In these roles, the relevant economic comparison changes. Gas peakers and diesel generators have low capital costs but high and volatile operating costs. Their marginal cost during peak hours can exceed $200 per megawatt-hour when fuel prices rise, emissions costs are internalised, or supply chains tighten. They are cheap to build but expensive to use. Large nuclear plants, by contrast, are cheap to run but expensive to build, and they are optimised for continuous operation rather than short, critical windows.
Small reactors sit awkwardly between these categories. They are not cheap baseload machines. Nor are they fuel-intensive marginal ones. Their economic case rests on replacing the most expensive and carbon-intensive slices of the system, not on minimising average generation costs. An SMR producing electricity at $100 – 120 per megawatt-hour looks uncompetitive next to a well-run gigawatt reactor, but it can undercut diesel generation and high-cost gas peaking once fuel, logistics and emissions are accounted for.
This distinction highlights a broader point that is often missed. Gas is not being replaced by a single technology. It is being eroded from multiple directions. Wind and solar attack gas on average energy cost, flooding the system with cheap electricity when conditions permit. Batteries attack gas on short-duration flexibility, smoothing intra-day fluctuations and shaving peaks. Demand response reduces peak amplitude by shifting or curtailing load. Grid expansion reduces spatial mismatches between supply and demand. Each of these tools removes part of gas’s value proposition, but none removes it entirely.
The residual role gas plays after these measures are deployed is small in energy terms but large in system importance. It provides firm, long-duration capacity when weather-dependent resources falter and demand refuses to cooperate. It sets prices during scarcity and anchors reliability. Replacing this residual requires a technology that can deliver power on demand for hours or days, independent of weather, geography and fuel supply chains.
This is where SMRs differ from many alternatives. They are not uniquely capable, but they combine attributes that are otherwise difficult to assemble in one asset: firm output, long-duration operation, compact physical footprint, and independence from local resource conditions. A battery can replace a gas peaker for one or two hours; beyond that, costs escalate sharply. Hydrogen can provide long-duration storage, but only with substantial conversion losses and infrastructure build-out. Hydropower does the job elegantly where geography allows, and not at all where it does not. SMRs offer firm capacity without requiring reservoirs, caverns or vast overbuilding of generation.
Several other well-known regularities reinforce this framing. One is Jevons paradox, first articulated in the 19th century: improvements in efficiency tend to increase, not decrease, total consumption. In energy systems, cheaper average electricity from renewables can raise demand and intensify peak stress rather than eliminate it. Another is Baumol’s cost disease, which explains why labour-intensive, safety-critical activities – such as nuclear construction – see costs rise relative to sectors where productivity can be automated away. These effects do not determine outcomes on their own, but they help explain why firm capacity remains valuable even as average costs fall.
Time and finance further complicate the comparison. Capital-intensive projects pay not only for materials and labour, but for patience. Interest during construction accumulates relentlessly. A large nuclear project that takes 12 to 15 years to complete can see its financial cost double before it produces electricity. A smaller project completed in three or four years accrues far less financing drag. Even with higher overnight costs per kilowatt, its all-in financial cost can be lower once time is priced properly. In systems with high interest rates, constrained balance sheets or political instability, this difference is not marginal.
Manufacturing realities reinforce this trade-off. Much of nuclear construction remains mature heavy industry, resistant to dramatic productivity gains. Yet some elements – digital control systems, factory-based quality assurance, repeatable fabrication steps – do offer learning effects that reduce risk and schedule uncertainty relative to bespoke megaprojects. These gains are insufficient to defeat the advantage of scale, but they matter where delay rather than efficiency is the binding constraint.
The political economy explains why these distinctions are often blurred. Officially, SMRs are framed as complements to large reactors. Practically, in many countries, they function as substitutes – not because they outperform gigawatt-scale plants, but because large plants have become institutionally difficult to deliver. Planning systems reward delay, litigation penalises decisiveness, and public trust in large infrastructure has eroded. Smaller projects fit more comfortably within administrative and electoral horizons, even if they deliver less electricity per dollar.
This substitution carries consequences. When SMRs replace diesel generation, gas peakers or fossil heat, they can reduce emissions and fuel exposure at tolerable cost. When they replace the idea of building large reactors altogether, they reduce the supply of firm clean power and raise average system costs. The technology has not failed; its role has changed.
Ontario illustrates the tension. The province’s cheapest clean electricity historically came from large reactors operating at high capacity factors, including those at Darlington Nuclear Generating Station. These plants exploited scale and longevity. Proposed SMRs, by contrast, are smaller and more expensive per unit of output. Their justification lies not in replicating Darlington’s economics, but in addressing specific constraints: replacing gas during peak hours, adding capacity incrementally, and reducing exposure to long construction timelines.
The debate over small nuclear reactors is often framed as a contest between efficiency and innovation. It is better understood as a contest between arithmetic and institutions. Large reactors remain the cheapest way to produce firm nuclear power when they can be built. Their advantage rests on scale, and no plausible combination of modularisation or learning can fully undo the loss incurred by shrinking them. That conclusion is mechanical, not ideological.
Yet infrastructure is not chosen on engineering cost alone. It is chosen under financial and political constraints that increasingly dominate outcomes. Large reactors concentrate risk: they require long construction periods, vast upfront commitments and tolerance for overruns that stretch across electoral cycles. Even when their expected cost is low, the distribution of outcomes is wide, and politics is averse to tails. Small reactors, by contrast, fragment that risk. Shorter build times reduce interest during construction, incremental deployment limits exposure, and smaller projects are easier to finance even at a modest premium.
This distinction matters because financing costs grow with time, not with megawatts. A reactor that is 20 – 30% more expensive to build but completed a decade sooner can rival – or even undercut – a cheaper design delayed by years of construction risk. In that sense, small reactors do not need to defeat the economics of scale to survive. They need only approach it closely enough that institutional friction does the rest.
That does not make small reactors a technological breakthrough. It makes them a political accommodation. They thrive not because they are more efficient, but because modern democracies have become less capable of underwriting large, long-lived projects. If that condition persists, slightly more expensive reactors that can be financed will beat cheaper ones that cannot. If it does not, scale will reassert itself. The arithmetic has not changed; the context has.
The arithmetic therefore supports a restrained conclusion. Small modular reactors will not be the cheapest way to generate large volumes of baseload electricity. The scaling relationship alone makes that unlikely. But they are not economically irrelevant. They occupy a narrow but consequential niche: replacing the most expensive, most carbon-intensive, and most politically entrenched uses of gas where other technologies struggle or become prohibitively costly.
Energy transitions falter when technologies are judged against the wrong benchmarks. SMRs look poor when compared with idealised large reactors built quickly and cheaply. They look more reasonable when compared with the diesel generators, gas peakers and fossil boilers that dominate the margins of the system. The danger lies in mistaking those margins for the centre.
Small modular reactors are neither a renaissance nor a distraction. They are a compromise between physical laws that reward scale and institutional realities that resist it. Used selectively, alongside renewables, storage, demand response and grids, they can accelerate decarbonisation where it would otherwise stall. Used indiscriminately, they risk shrinking ambition without solving the underlying problem. The mathematics does not dictate policy, but it does narrow the space in which honest choices can be made.
